entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Okay, we are ruling and we are doing the video interview with Ankit for Greenway. And I'm going to start with the questions now. So, Ankit, could you please introduce yourself and your role at the company?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yeah. Hi, I'm Ankit Mathur. I'm co founder and CEO of Greenway. We started Greenway in 2010, my co founder and I, and it's a company that works in energy access with two broad business areas, clean kitchens and renewable and efficient power. Our main business has been selling improved cookstoves to rural households to replace inefficient and unclean and unhealthy wood burning or charcoal burning stoves.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: What products or services do you offer and how do you contribute to improving the food?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: So, as I mentioned, the main business that we have is selling improved cookstoves. The idea of the product is to replace rudimentary mud stoves or open fires on which people cook food. And food, as we all know, is probably in a lot of cases, the most important thing for which we live. Some people live to eat, some people eat to live, but in either case you need to cook. And we believe that getting people to cook in a more healthy manner is essential to the basic parameters of better quality of life. And when we set up Greenway, we set it up with an idea that we'll design and distribute products for increasing the quality of life for rural customers. And that's precisely what we do. And the problem that we solve is that of clean cooking or lack of access to cleaner cooking. And that's essential to food and agrisystems.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: What would you like to achieve at Creedway?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: We would like to remain relevant as a company from the point of view of designing products, from the point of view of reaching a large number of customers. And to do that, we constantly have to innovate, we constantly have to evolve in terms of what we offer our customers. With changing consumer preferences, we do see that people have moved from unclean or rudimentary mud stoves, open fires to improve cookstoves, improved cookstoves, burning firewood or charcoal. And now we see a move towards even cleaner systems which are either electric or LPG based. And in the same manner, what we want to do is to keep innovating to keep the kitchens cleaner or to push the envelope further in terms of going towards completely renewable forms of cooking that may, if I have to describe it, it could involve generating electricity from solar, storing it in battery packs that are recyclable, and then using it in induction based cookstoves that could have 90, 95% efficiency to cook food in a manner that has no emissions either at the point of use or at the point of generation.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: What obstacles have you encountered and how.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Have you managed in general? Starting up a new hardware business is difficult, be it in India or anywhere in the world. And no doubt we also faced similar obstacles in the early part of setting up. And as we overcame that obstacle, we also faced other challenges related to scaling up the business, which could relate to sales and distribution, access to capital for scaling the business, as well as access to capital to our customers. At different points in time, we've had different angels coming in and helping us. So be it. Real angel investors who helped us get capital to scale up the business, to set up our manufacturing supply chain in order, or partnerships with financial institutions wherein we could convince them to give micro loans to end customers to buy this product that we sell, which sells for about 30, $35 to the end customer. And of course, you know, whenever you can get help from policymakers in terms of having good policies that enable us to sort of increase access to cleaner cooking.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Whom do you work and collaborate with?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: So we work and collaborate with a number of partners, mostly to address issues that we cannot address ourselves, be it manufacturing or prototyping, where we work with a variety of vendors, be it carbon projects, where we work with offsetters as well as intermediaries to finance subsidies for end customers. We work extensively with non banking financial companies in India that give loans to our end customers who then buy the product and then are able to return the money to the NBFC in small installments. And most importantly, we work with our end customers, end users who help us understand how to make the product better, how to deliver it better, how to deliver the message of the importance of the product better, and and also our employees who keep us on our toes to make sure that we deliver a better product and reach more and more customers.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: What is your advice to people who would want to do the same thing?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: I would like to say that one needs to be very frugal and very innovative when trying to solve problems that require behavior change because it takes time before consumers and communities get the message and start acting on it. And it also requires to get a lot of people together to establish partnerships that will help you set things up, be patient and help you just get over tough times where things may seem low, but you know, there could be a call or you could call a friend or a partner to solve a problem that you would find difficult to do yourself. And finally, I would say that you need to be ambitious and raise capital ambitiously, not be very frugal when you're raising capital. And these problems need to be amplified and told to investors. And I'm sure somebody will write a good check for solving these. Thank you.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: So welcome Ankit to the Socioscope interview. Thanks for agreeing to take part. I wonder if we could, if you could start us off by saying, by introducing your company and telling us a little bit about when you started it. Share with us some history, the legal structure and who did you start with?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Sure. First of all, thank you for making the trek all the way to Noida. Pretty much the edge and you know, go a few kilometers further, you'll be reaching Meerut soon. All right. Yeah. So coming back to the company, in fact, starting from that standpoint, I started Greenway with my co founder Neha junaija back in 2010. The company is in the business of energy access, predominantly on clean cooking energy, but we are slowly expanding into electricity and power management with an idea that clean cooking would possibly or probably or much faster than that, reach electricity based clean cooking in the years to come. We started the company in 2010, as I said, with the broad idea that better products can make quality of life better in rural areas. We saw white space in designing products for these rural households. And one of the first problems that we identified was that of lack of access to cleaner cooking. Just before starting up Greenway, we were in the business of renewable energy and carbon credits consulting. So that took us to a lot of remote areas where people would offer food or chai, and typically it was made on a mud stove using firewood. And we thought that this seems like a fairly simple problem to solve from a technical standpoint. And given that both of us are engineers, we thought why don't we design something and start selling and that's how the company started and we'll talk more about it.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Sure. I think it would be interesting before I go on to the next question on my list to actually sort of define what the problem was that you were trying to solve. Why is clean cooking, why are appliances enabling clean cooking so important in India, especially rural India? Sure, sure.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: So India, as you know, is still an lmic, which means that we are a low middle income country and India has high income disparity as well. Large part of the population still is dependent on agriculture for income and the income is rather bunched up. It's not regular income where you can really plan, spend and buy things based on credit. That comes easily. So when it comes to cooking, the government now has been pushing LPG quite a bit, which is somewhat a fairly clean point of view source for cooking. But of course it's a fossil fuel. But we can keep talking about it as far as the potential health benefits of cleaner Air at the point of use are concerned. LPG is a good solution for a large number of households. Unfortunately, back when we started, and even today as we go in these areas where we try to sell our stoves, we see that most people are unable to keep buying these LPG cylinders. And in spite of the best intentions of the government, policymakers and possibly the household members themselves, they're unable to have a clean source of cooking which adds a monthly or a regular cost to their household budgets. So wood is available for free. Most of these people are living in, you know, living close to fields or forests where they can collect fuel for free. And to add to that, just before we started the interview, we were talking about the poor aqi, you know, in this place that we are living in. And close to a quarter of that PM2.5 is coming from biomass based cooking at households that we try to cater to. And unless you address that, forget about the external air quality, the household air pollution by itself would end up killing a lot of people, which it does. Different studies have estimated that anywhere between 3 to 4 million people die because of things that could be attributed to cooking on firewood or charcoal or something like that. And it continues to remain a problem in India, as in sub Saharan Africa and some other parts of the world.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Right. Would it be fair to assume that there is a gender bias as well, that women are disproportionately affected because they primarily.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yes, yes. So I would say that if men would have done the cooking, I would have been a billionaire by now for making this product, or somebody else would have been 50 years ago because this product would have sold a lot better. So imagine somebody in 2010 not doing something related to cybersecurity, but making a cookstove that sells for $30 to end customers who can't pay. So I think there's definitely a gender bias in terms of whether women can spend, whether they have the agency to buy, and even when they would want to move up the ladder, whether they can do it or not. And a lot of these things are related to whether they are the ones who are making the money, whether they are the ones who have control on the household budgets. Culturally, does the society look down on women who make independent decisions and stuff like that? So one of the reasons why we've sold better in southern India and northeastern India is because generally gender equality is better in these parts of the country. Try selling in the Indo Gangetic plains.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: That's a very interesting point. Thanks for pointing that out. How does it look in Africa compared.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: With India, I think Africa in general has better gender equality.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Than India. Again, you know, one should not paint Africa as a whole. Right. We have worked in very few countries in Africa. We've worked in Kenya, Tanzania, Zambia, parts of Malawi, parts of Zimbabwe maybe, and, and we've done some pilots in Ghana and Nigeria.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: So primarily English speaking Africa.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Primarily English speaking Africa. We would love to work in drc, but yet to make inroads into that market. So in these countries, what I see is that if women have money, they are able to spend it without a societal pressure, so to say in general, the customers or potential customers that we deal with have very little money. So I'm not quite sure how it works in slightly more, you know, well to do populations or urban populations, but it looks better than India.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Okay, interesting. So let's go back to your founding story.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yes.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: You told me about NEHA and how you started the company together and you started in 2010 and I was in my second year of college back then, so that's a long time ago. So what have been or who have been, who are, what have they been your main enablers and obstacles and what would you consider to be your most noteworthy milestones?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Alright, in the very early stages, the biggest stumbling block was the inability to prototype what we envisioned, which continues to be a problem in India, which is that early stage prototyping and getting a product from concept to a realized physical product, it just takes a very long time. And now fast forward 15 years, we have a factory, we have teams and everything, it still takes very long. So I don't know whether it is to do with the equipment, with the manpower, or whether it's to just the inability of people to take that risk in terms of actually putting things together themselves. So that was and continues to be one of the major stumbling blocks. But once we made the product, the biggest challenge has been distribution. Something that we just spoke about, the fact that you're trying to sell it to consumers, A, who don't have regular household budgets, B you're trying to sell to women who typically don't control the budgets and C, it's a product that's not really known and understood by even the customers themselves. So it's very difficult to sell it to a channel that would eventually sell it to the end customer. So if I point out the problems these have been the problems that have kind of held us back, in most cases, the enablers have been a few good people with good intentions. I think that is something that keeps us going. We were in the early years, we used to participate a lot in these national international business plan competitions. That was a thing back then before the VC check signing became a thing. Right. And we were possibly the first winners of Intel Global Challenge from India, which gave us loads of money. We were quite surprised that we made it that far and used a lot of that money too. For the initial setting of operations of the company, there's an entity called Global alliance for Clean Cookstoves that has become the Clean Cooking alliance now that gave us a grant in 2014, which was helpful in setting up the factory that we have in Gujarat. And even before that, we had a few angel investors who helped us, including CIIE, I am Ahmedabad, and Mr. Sundar Hemrajani, who at one point in time.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Used to work at times.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Subsequently we raised some capital from Akshay Ventures and Acumen. Acumen has been, I think, a leading voice in terms of things to do in the hard to reach markets, frontier markets, whatever you call them. And so at different points in time, we've had something or the other help us get over any capital bottlenecks that we were facing. But the challenge remains lack of access to finance to large number of potential customers. Imagine a 3000 rupee product. How difficult can it be to pay for it if it's financed? Right. Unfortunately, 3,000 rupees of finance is not available to most of our customers.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Okay, okay. There are a couple of interesting issues that you, that you raised probably in the past five minutes and we'll get back to them. One is of course the. What is what you just said, which is, you know, the availability of credit.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: In rural India.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Which is a barrier. The second is distribution challenges, both production and distribution. How do you get a product ready for market? How do you get a prototype to something that's market ready? And how do you solve the gender inequality and behavior change problems? We can get into that. But for now let's just let me bring the focus back to the organization. Would you mind explaining the organization structure? There's you and Neha. So who else is there? What are the divisions and support functions?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Sure, sure. So I must point out that Neha is no longer, you know, an active executive at the company anymore.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: So.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: So she was at greenway till 2022 and now she runs an NBFC that is a peer to peer NBFC that lends money to possibly the same segment or slightly creamier segment of what we try to sell to at Greenway. More urban, of course, because all of that. So now it's me and there's a bunch of talented individuals who manage different verticals within the company. We have our first employee, Shwetazi, who is, who is a part COO and part cfo. But he wants to take on the CFO journey. So we'll see how that goes. There's Saurabh Saraf, who is. Is a junior of mine from Delhi College of Engineering where I did my engineering. He did that same thing a few years hence. And he manages the commercial side of things. So basically the sales and marketing team reports into him, the accounts team reports into Shoaib. There's a head of supply chain who reports to Shoaib, but who also works with me closely because I double up as the CTO as well for the company. And the head of supply chain comes with a lot of experience from the Tata group. So he's an experienced professional who has been trying to keep manufacturing operations spick and span and making sure that we produce, we have the talent at that particular facility. So these are the key kind of functions and team members we're still working on trying to figure out how to grow the sales and marketing team and how to bolster the RD team that in the initial years was pretty much working with me and that is now finding it rather difficult to work autonomously. So we need to find that organization now within Greenway.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: If you're getting a call, I can pause.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yeah, yeah, that would be great.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Okay, so we're back. So we've just discussed, you know, the history of your organization, the structure, key enablers, obstacles, milestones and a good start. Can we go in depth into what are your business activities? You are a for profit organization.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yes.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: What are your, what are your activities, what do you sell, what do you produce, whom do you sell it to? Tell me a little bit about that business model and if you could describe those operations in detail.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Sure. So we manufacture and we sell. We design, manufacture and sell. We started out with improved cookstoves which we still sell. Greenway Smart stove and Greenway Jumbo Stove. Smart Stove was our first product. Greenway Jumbo Stove has been our best selling product and still sells at different points in time. We innovated a few other things in the household appliances space that we did launch. For commercial reasons, we could not keep up with manufacturing and distributing them. By 2014, we had set up our manufacturing facility in Bhadotara. Between 2011 and 14, we worked with various vendors to produce Greenway Smart Stove and sell it to, I would call them middle class, rural customers. So it's not the poorest of the poorest poor in rural areas that buy our products. It's typically not the sarpanch who needs the product. It's the middle class in rural areas that takes our products which we want to cater to. And typically it's this middle class that also is the target for self help groups, for MFIs, NBS, because similar to urban middle class, rural middle class also wants social mobility, economic mobility and is trying to save, is trying to get the kids educated, trying to find a better life and trying to put together schemes and things that would work for them. And in terms of how this happens, we, we, as I said, are an integrated operation. So we buy the raw materials, we manufacture the stoves, we have logistics and warehouses capability in terms of having warehouses pretty much in five states now across eight locations it was a little more, but with GST coming in, we have needed lesser warehouses. So that has been more economical. And then unfortunately we've not been very successful in setting up a dealer distributor network, which means that we have to reach our customers ourselves in terms of marketing, in terms of the delivery of the product. So we also have a fairly extensive method of identifying the customers, getting to them, recording that data as to who the customer is. So we also have a fairly well versed IT team that handles a lot of data. So we would have, you know, anonymized and anonymized Data for about 1 1/2 million customers of the total 3 million stores that we've sold till date.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: So I don't know how many parts of the questions did I answer, but no.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Yeah, we can break that down. So I'd like to take a step back. So you've innovated a bunch of things, but in terms of your product lines today, would I be right in saying that there are two main cookstove types, is that correct?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: More recently, in fact, more recently, we've started selling other kitchen appliances that would go with the stoves. But that's a training operation. We buy from somebody and we sell it to those same customers. We've also started, as I earlier mentioned, now Greenway is not just clean cooking or clean kitchens. It's clean kitchens and renewable and efficient power. So in the last one year we made a conscious switch towards going into electricity access, but we've started that business from a very different angle. We are not starting from rural households. We've started with putting up rooftop solar in urban societies because we saw white space in terms of urban societies taking up solar rooftops. The challenge there is that these middle income consumers within urban areas live in cooperative housing societies where collective decisioning delays or prevents solar rooftops from happening. One of the reasons for that is lack of access to capital. So we have started installing these on a Resco model and we believe that this kind of paves way for us to work towards electric cooking in urban areas. And how this will move to electric cooking in rural areas we don't quite know yet. But that's the other kind of business line. And related to that we also sell, sell certain efficient lighting products in both urban and rural areas.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: So when you say Resco, is it kind of like what does that refer.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: To as Renewable energy service company. So we invest in the asset and we own the asset and we sell units of electricity generated and these societies get the net metering benefit and at a predefined time we hand over the asset to them. So typically the asset once we paid ourselves back. So we do a 10 year PPA, a power purchase agreement and this equipment life is typically 25 years.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: 25 years, yeah. Okay, that's very interesting. So what percentage so you've taken and I wouldn't say you've pivoted, you've added a business life. So we'll get into financials in detail a bit. Just in. Just a bit. But what percentage of your overall business is this new business?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: So at this point it's less than 10%. But before see that electricity and power management might become close to 40% of our business in the next five years. So we find that it's, it's a different market segment, it's a different driver. There are different drivers of success in that market segment and it kind of de risks us from all the vagaries of external shocks that rural customers have to deal with.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Sure, sure.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Right.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: The International Solar alliance is in session in Delhi right now.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Oh, is it okay?

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: I think it's on till tomorrow. Tomorrow the 30th, right?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: I saw some posters maybe look back. Very interesting. So you spoke about, okay, you spoke about the product line and you've given some useful context about the additional business line. You earlier talked about the prototyping challenge and the distribution challenge Challenge. So let's look. Prototyping we've understood. But distribution, I said we'd come back to it. So let's talk a little bit about distribution. You say that you haven't been able to establish a dealership sort of network and you have to talk to your customer, the end user directly. That sounds like a bit of a challenging. Ask so how did you solve that challenge?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Well, we are fairly densely present in certain areas and we are just not present in other areas. So in other areas, we just do B2B business. For example, you heard a phone call that came in and there's a B2B customer who will in turn either sell or distribute whatever he or she wants to do it. But in most other areas where we operate, we have a B2B2C kind of a model wherein this middle B either provides us financing or works with us to get some subsidies in place, or works with us on carbon credits and ensures that there are subsidies and to service these markets, then we set up warehouses, we have a vehicle network. We have large number of people on street who go from door to door, or let's say village to village, do demonstrations, gather women people, whatever, show the product and try to sell the product either at full price with finance or at a subsidized price without finance. So that's been the model, which is why it is critical for us to find that middle collaborator.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: What is the typical profile of the middle collaborator? Is it an nbfc?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yeah. So when it's finance, it's typically an nbfc. Okay. Where the NBFC would give the money to the end customer and would collect it and we get our price up front.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Are we talking about large national players like Sriram Finance or local players?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: So Sriram Finance is mostly on vehicle finance and secured loans. We're talking about unsecured credit here because you can't really hypothecate a cook stove. Right. And so you're talking. We typically work very well with smaller players who are local, who have a good reach, and who have some incentive to keep working with the same community and keep that, you know, engagement on. So strong regional players work very well for us. There have, there have been some big names, but. But as they've become bigger, this, this business has become more and more irrelevant for them. Okay. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Okay. And also you say if it's finance, that's the value being mba. What about other.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: So, so for, for, for a fair amount of time, let's say 2020 onwards, up to 2023 or early 2024, the voluntary carbon markets were very strong. And these cook stoves reduce emissions rather economically. Right. And if you mark out a territory and you register a project with one of the voluntary carbon registries, it's possible for you to verify these projects at different points in time and generate carbon offsets that could potentially be sold to end offsetters. Now, there could be a financial structure. And we've been successful in using these structures rather well in which you could do a forward sale of the credits and get a prepay and against the forward sale that you do. And that prepay could be used for subsidizing the stove that you end up distributing.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Right.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: So a 3000 rupees stove could be made as cheap as 500 or 1000 rupees. And these 20002500 rupees are paid for from the money that has been given by an offsetter or an intermediary who believes that these credits would be worth a lot more when they are generated. So that would be a typical subsidy partner.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Okay, that is very clear.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Great.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: And I also had a question about the replacement rate. So I mean this is. What do you, what do you, how long do you. When somebody buys. When your typical customer buys a cookstove from you, how long do they keep.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Lasts very long. So we give a two year replacement warranty. So for two years nothing happens. The product is made of stainless steel, aluminium jacket, and there's some amount of paint that also prevents any rusting, even though stainless steel is quite resistant to rusting. But one must remember that somebody is actually cooking firewood that has all sorts of impurities. It's not a processed fuel. So it's quite likely that the product would go bad. Having said all of that, the product typically lasts six to seven years unless left out in the open, especially in coastal areas where the air is more saline. In Rajasthan, for example, we have users who've been using the product for nine years. Yeah, yeah, maybe. Maybe some even longer. But last I checked, there were users who had been using the same product with some amount of servicing done for nine years.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Okay, so that means when the replacement rate. So when like 1/7 of the 1/7 or 1/8 or 1/9 of the cook stoves in operation are being replaced every.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Year, that means they're not. Because it's not necessary that the people end up buying the same cookstove again from me, or if I really know whose stove is going bad and if they're living in the same place and, or if they're ready to pay the same amount of money or more money, because someone who bought the cookstove nine years ago would have bought it at a much lower price given all the inflation that's been going on and you know, the spikes in commodities prices.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: No, but from a business growth perspective.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: So from a business growth perspective, it's very difficult for us to really look for lifetime Value from selling cookstore version one to version two to version three. It's quite unlike a mobile phone in that sense. So we have to. Which is why I mentioned that we started selling certain other kitchen appliances as well. Well, more rather rudimentary pressure cookers mixer that could be sold in addition to the same customers or to similar customers in the vicinity.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Okay, okay, that's, that's clear.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: So let's talk a little bit about how the organization is funded and a little bit of the financial sustainability. You did talk about raising capital in the pre VC era. It was very interesting. You said you have participated in innovation competitions. Tell me a little bit about that journey and the capital structure. Sure.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: So we are a fairly closely held company, NEHA and I, who still own more than 85% of the company and certain key employees own the rest. And the angels own about 6, 7% now in the company. So in the very early years, which is let's say pre2010, there were very few VCs operating in this space. Also, impact investment wasn't a term back then. Right. Something that's become quite prevalent in, in the last four or five years. Right. Which is also tapering off again with external factors which we may not talk about. But in terms of managing the money, early on it was mostly from these kind of thrift competitions, whatever you could get, whatever you could gather and trying to make some money selling stoves.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Sorry, go on. Excuse me.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: So, so it, so, so we quickly understood that it's, it's not one of those businesses that will show 100x growth anytime soon. And it's a, it's a long haul old economy business which, which works more in terms of manufacturing, distribution. There's a lot of impact. The impact is upsized, but there's not enough money. Right.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Not every kind of business needs to be or should be VC funded because once it's VC funded it has to take on a very different. There are limits to what a VC funded company can do.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yes, yes, that's true. And especially when it comes to impact.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Because.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: As you had mentioned earlier, a term behavioral change. Right. So behavioral change takes a while to happen. It could take a generation, it could happen faster. I mean, you don't, you know how many early adopters would still be sticking to a mud stove? Right. They would have adopted some technology faster and moved up.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Right.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: So one of the challenges was that while we did not have access to venture capital early on we understood that revenue is where we have to make our money from. So we became Profitable very early on. By 2013 we were profitable and that profit was enough to sustain a small team. It was definitely not enough to set up a factory for which GACC gave us the money, or at least we used the money that they gave us for marketing to set up a factory and said that this is better spend than marketing. And then by 2015 we were in talks with Acumen and they were kind enough to write us a check along with Asha Impact. Back then it was about one and a half million dollars. And then later in the voluntary carbon markets spike era, which lasted very short window of opportunity, which was from 2020 to 2024, I think we were able to underwrite a lot of subsidy from these carbon credits for almost a million stoves, which was substantial. And now, you know, given that we have recently ventured into more of an asset ownership kind of a business, we believe that now and the company has a long history and a positive net worth and financial stability, we can potentially look at debt as a source of capital, which we are actively looking at. And I think with expansion of the product portfolio, expansion of the service portfolio and de risking the overall revenue pockets, I believe that we could potentially also look at VCs or impact investors. Again, that we are not limited only to rural households for clean kitchens. That's one line of business. There's another renewable and efficient power and that's exploding every day. Right, Right. So that's, that's how we are.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Okay. Your. Is there somewhere on your website where I can access your financials?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: No, but you can. I'll send them across to you. Yeah, they are, they are accessible on mca. All, all Indian private limited companies have their financials there if they've been doing their filings and if they're not, you should not be interviewing them.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: So I mean.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: So I mean your, so your revenue split today.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: You said is you're a profitable business today.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Your revenue split split is 90% the traditional kitchen cookstoves or other kitchen appliances and 10% the new business line.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: So, so I must clarify, it's. It's 90% cook stoves, kitchen appliances and the carbon that was generated because. So for example, last year when the MFI business took a hit, a lot of our sales took a hit. But because carbon takes a while to generate and then sell, there was that late glow that kept us profitable. And with good numbers, we're struggling to see what will happen in 26. Right. But we are hoping that the other new parts of the businesses and the distribution network that we've started working on since the MFIs took a hit. Will kind of keep the boat afloat.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: All right. Okay. Okay, great. I think that that should be good enough for this section.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: I'll take a break.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Okay. We are resuming. Yeah, it looks good. So you all, I already asked you to define the problem early on because my next question was going to be, what need do you feel? What problem do you address? You've spoken about that, but perhaps we could then move on to your motivation, your source of motivation. Why do this? Why run this kind of business? Social business?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: What motivates you and what are your.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Values and what is your purpose?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yeah, that's a difficult one. I think at some level I did not want to become a consultant. Coming out of a B school. I went to imm6 to 8 and yeah, I just did not understand the utility of consultants. I do that better now. Now that I have my own company, I do understand that they do add value. I couldn't see myself moving from one project to another and leaving whatever impact could have been to whoever is the implementer. That's one. I definitely did not want to be a banker. I still don't see their value, given how fintech has taken off. I think that their value is in making sure to position central banks. Allow them to remain by making fintechs, you know. Yeah, by keeping them at bay. Right. So that takes two of the best career opportunities in a sense.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Right.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: But even as an entrepreneur, why get into something that has a social impact? I think, to be honest, it gives you some level of satisfaction in terms of being able to cause a positive change. And in my kind of business, where you have a manufacturing setup and distribution and logistics in rural areas, I do feel that that change is stickier, as in if you build this infrastructure and people get an understanding of what advantage that infrastructure could have, that positive change will travel further compared to, let's say, if you have an app on which you can buy something faster. So I believe that the ability to make a positive change that will last slightly longer keeps me in this business. Right. I mean, that's why we went from selling cookstoves to setting up, you know, solar rooftops. And so, and, and when we started setting up these solar rooftops, everyone said, why are you doing such small projects? It's 50 kilowatts, 100 kilowatts. People are talking 15 megawatts. And we're like, this is a white space. This is a segment that will continue to grow and this infrastructure will. Will be a positive journey in terms of an energy transition to consumers who would otherwise not make that decision. Right. So I think that ability to make some kind of infrastructure and that having a positive change is what keeps me in this business. That was the idea when we named our company Greenway Grameen Infra. Right. So that sustainable future cannot just be in terms of a quick service intervention. It has to be in terms of permanent infrastructure that people can continue to use. And then we see investing in cleaner kitchens as an infrastructure that allows your household to be cleaner, healthier, allows you to cook in an environment which is healthier. Right. Etc. Etc.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: All right, all right. So were there any, you know, in, in your path, have there been any external influences behind your decision to do this? And, and in turn, in creating this business, have you inspired, inspired others? And I'm not asking about competition. Like I asked this question to Iki and the founder told me I've inspired all my competitors. So has anybody else thought, okay, this is something that I want to get into as well. I want to work in this space either as an NGO or a business.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: I doubt that this is a difficult space to be. I personally believe that we are one of the longest surviving companies in the sector that has remained true to what they make and sell. There have been companies that have been funded through, through either corporate grants and then when the corporate grant run dry, they had to change their path. Or there have been others that have been funded through VCs or PEs. And then when that ran dry, you know, people decided to shutter. But for some reason we remained here. So I doubt anyone has started a clean cooking business. But I must say that in terms of, in terms of the partnerships that we've had, there's that mutual inspiration that flows both ways. I learned from my partners running Greenway is how NEHA decided to be in the fintech space, that, okay, we are providing products, but what if we provide cash to these women? Right. And is there an alternative way of doing it?

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: We were interrupted by rogue phone call. So if you could just go back 10 seconds and repeat what you just said. It's recording. I can verify that it's recording. So just repeat what I was saying.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: So saying that I believe that we have been inspired by our partners and by being in this business, we have learned a lot about other businesses and it has increased our ability to conceptualize and deliver other products and services better. So the example that I was giving was my co founder who started a fintech startup now and trying to find a new source of capital to Fund similar households, similar micro entrepreneurs in a manner that they could run their lives better.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Right. You did say there was a slight difference between her target segment and your target segment.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yes.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: So we're not looking at a situation where her company is subsidizing your.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Unfortunately not. Yeah. I mean, I would love that to happen where people can subsidize stores and I can sell more.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Make perfect sense.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: It would make perfect sense. Right. But yeah, it doesn't work like that. I mean, in most cases, NBFCs find it very difficult to give these micro loans. And at this point, banks have stopped lending anything below 2 lakh directly, except on credit card.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Right.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: And for that you need to be in a completely different.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Got it. Okay, so let's now talk about. I think you've already described how the company has changed over time. So I was going to ask you about changes. Let's sort of move. Let's move into. Let's put the focus back on the organization itself. I'd like to talk about challenges. You have outlined certain challenges. For example, the prototyping, distribution, the difficulty of working in the space in general, the fact you can't scale very quickly. There's a lot of hard work to be done. Any other challenges that you would like to highlight?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: I think the fact that we are growing in this country but probably not growing fast enough or as we are growing, the income disparity seems to be increasing, not decreasing, is a big macroeconomic challenge. And that affects SMEs like us that are particularly focused on. On income groups that are not seeing any real growth in their income. So every few years the formerly employed people have an increase in incomes. But India has such a huge informally employed population which is either unemployed or unemployed, but we don't quite know and they are not seeing any real wage income growth. That's one challenge. The other challenge that I see is lack of macro data. The government has been rather.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: I would say.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: What is the right word? Slow in collecting and disseminating data. Yeah. Census is one such example. I, I still try to work with 2011 census.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Yeah, I keep saying that's going to happen.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: It's going to happen.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: It's not happening.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Okay, great. So we. I've asked you about challenges. Yes. What, what about achievements? What do you see as your biggest achievements? Achievements?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yeah, I think, I think the fact that we remain solvent for so long just feels like a victory every year, you know, so. And the fact that there's a lot of talented people that have worked with us and who have to Move on. Because, you know, this is. This is not a company that will make so much money to keep giving them that upward salary that certain other companies or industries can. Right. So it's great to be friends with them, to sort of share notes as they go through their careers and also sort of get young professionals and see them sometimes thrive, sometimes survive, whatever they're going through here. And I think providing that platform to. To a large number of people feels like an achievement.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: All right, fair enough. I wonder if we could sort of go a bit deeper and do a SWOT analysis.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: All right. So I can type as you talk.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Or we could also use the board.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: I would need it to.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yes, sure.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: I need to put it up on the screen so I can type. But if you think that writing makes. You have to think better, you could.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yeah, yeah, I'll take that option and you may type.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Or I could simply take a photograph.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Oh, yeah, sure, whatever.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Whatever works.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Right. So what would you say are your strengths?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yes, I think we are very frugal as an organization, which has helped us survive. Right. We are quite innovative when it comes to both product innovations as well as financial innovations. That has allowed us to grow at different points in time. We are quite patient and we are also perseverant at the same time. So that has helped us again, kind of keep plowing through whatever comes our way. I think I'll just pause there. In terms of the strengths, in terms of weaknesses, I think we've not learned enough sales and marketing, and that has been a persistent weakness. From an organizational perspective, I would call it lack of external stakeholder management. That's rather long, but that's what it is. We have at times we've been inflexible in terms of either reducing quality to offer a lower price or, you know, not being flexible enough to work with government officers. Right. Or ministers or whatever you may call it. Right. So we've been inflexible there. That has taken away some the of opportunities. And we don't come from rich families, actually. Yeah. Or a business background for that matter. So that's the weakness. We are not able to, you know, sort of just plow in our own money to keep the venture going and feel good about it. So those are the strengths, those are the weaknesses. What are the opportunities ahead? I would say that changing consumer preferences are an opportunity because we do feel that consumers do want to move up the energy ladder. They do want to save costs on how much they're spending, both in terms of time, et cetera. Time and money, there's a recognition of the fact that this is a problem that needs to be addressed by policymakers. Right.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: So.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: There'S more awareness, let's call it that, in external stakeholders. And there's, I think, I think fintech and climate finance is a huge opportunity that allows for alternative sources of financing being made available for these sorts of energy transitions. Right, right. And in terms of threats, I would say, you know, I would call exacerbating income equal. Because when income equality comes down, then people who are at the middle to low to bottom don't believe that anything that they'll do will actually make their lives better. So even though they may believe that, okay, this is a good product, but what difference does it make? I'm still going to be wretched. So they will not buy anything. There's, I would say, slow progress on climate action. This is a big problem. I mean, now I've been watching or waiting for Paris Agreement climate markets to come about. It's been 10 years. Yeah, I've been waiting, you know, and now they don't even have the curo protocol markets working. So kind of in the soup. What else? I think sometimes external shocks in general. So these could be policy induced shocks, for example, demonetization in India.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Right.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Or let's say US or Europe pulls out of the Paris Agreement climate markets. That's going to be a big shock, whatever remains. Right. So yeah, I'll just pause there.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: All right, that was very crisp. I've got all of that. I'll take a picture of that later as well.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yeah, not very nicely written, but nice to have.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Very useful. That was really crisp and I think it also rehashes quite a few things. Some things that you said earlier in the talk. Right. So I want to just interject with a quick organization question, going back to larger philosophical questions. How do you know it's a fairly small team that you work at is the leadership team. How do you make decisions about changes in your organization?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yeah, that's, you know, till the time that the co founders were working together, it was still a little more collaborative and we had that back and forth. Now it's, it's become a little more autocratic. I'm the autocrat here. Right.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: So you don't seem very autocratic.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: No, I'm not at all. Which is a problem. Which is why these decisions take very long. By the time I get, you know, these functional leaders to understand what exactly it is that they need to decide and change and move on, it takes a while. So I sometimes feel that I'm trying to put on a coaching mentorship hat when I don't want to or need to, that's a bit of a problem. So it's mostly functional areas. And given that it's an old company now, older people have gotten slightly more calcified in the silos that they've been working in. So as an entrepreneur, I'm fine, right. I can get in, get out, into anything and I'm the boss, so I can do that. But to get those leaders to talk fluidly with each other is the bigger decision making problem. So if the decision is to be taken by me and just pushed through, that's fine. But if you're going to get four people in, in the room and, and get them to contribute and then decide that has been taking longer.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Okay, okay. That's quite interesting. When you say so I like to use of the word calcified. It's a very visual sort of thing. So you're saying that, you know, do you mean by that that they are now sort of set in their way and they're resistant to new ideas?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: I would think that, yeah. And you see, typically it's the non consumer facing functions that get into that trap more easily. But I must say that with success, even consumer facing functions might think that whatever I'm doing is leading to success and so I don't need to change which I've seen happen. You know, I, and, and we've had some of the people, you know, move on because of that.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Okay, interesting. I would, I would then ask you. So you, you told me earlier that you've actually not seen a lot of people, you know, come into this space and succeed. Your, your, your unique kid that you one of the longest running organizations. So what are the critical skills and knowledge required to do what you've done to succeed?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: I think that goes back to the SWOT analysis that we did. You need to be innovative and you need to be very, very frugal. Right? You need to somehow manage to do things with lot lesser means than you thought were available to you. So that's what we managed to do.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: For so long.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: And make the best use of opportunities that come your way. So in the early years when we saw that our packaging, our product could be seen as aspirational to consumers, we completely went with that and we, we really invested in better packaging which meant that alright, when people saw it, they felt better about opening it and everything. So I think that has allowed us to remain relevant and I can see that we've been doing the same thing for a decade and it's time to really do something different soon. Otherwise we might also join the list of people who went out of business trying to, you know, make clean, cleaner kitchens. What.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: So could you sort of, you spoke, you spoke about the new business line that's coming up, the opportunity in Fintech and Clamax. So if you could paint a picture of your ideal future, what would your goals look like? What would that future look like?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: I think if I could. We've recently invested in a new factory pretty much in the vicinity of the old one. So I believe that if I could set up a platform that could continue innovating in, in the climate action space, because that was something that was the initial space that we came in from, into cooking and, and, and being in the sector for so long. I've been exposed to so many different kinds of projects starting from, you know, regenerative agriculture all the way to, you know, enhanced rock weathering or, or, or you know, the, the middle, that is energy efficiency, be it at industrial level or household level as we do. I believe that if I could set up, if I could set up a platform that continues to do this without worrying too much about, you know, what do we have to do, how much do we have to sell next quarter to remain relevant and that would be a great achievement. To my mind, I would be quite satisfied with that because that would, that would mean that we've really been able to take action for a better future in a manner.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Right.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Which is, which is a great feeling to sort of sip your beer with. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Nice. And what criteria do you use to measure success as a leader? There are Obviously the financial KPIs that you look at as a leader, but you know, you as a leader, when you look at your organization, are there any metrics that you yourself have devised to measure how well the organization is doing?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Well, particularly I am a big believer in, in being able to provide stable jobs because that's something that is missing in India mostly. I believe that we need a lot more jobs. I'm still creating jobs at a very, very low scale. But I like it when our employees, A, the overall number grows and B, when one of them or a few of them able to invest in a better quality of life. So I've seen our, you know, there was a time when we set up the factory and a lot of people used to cycle their way to work. And then, you know, in a few years time I saw that everyone had a bike and then some people had very good bikes. And then, you know, somebody said that, oh, I bought a house. And I was like, wow, you bought a house.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Wow.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Okay, nice. And then there have been demonstrators in the field who have kind of risen up the ranks and, you know, again, cycle to pike, bike to car, maybe a house, something like that. And that is a fuzzy measure of success. But that tells you that other than you, somebody is happy. Right. And I think that's very important because whether. Whether the organization is or will be is not entirely sure. But, you know, if people are happier, they'll possibly collaborate better.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Okay, very nice. Very nice. I believe it's a really nice way of thinking. I believe I got a similar answer from the founder of IKEA as well.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: It gave him satisfaction to see that people had grown.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yeah. Lovely.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: And perhaps my last question before we move on to the stakeholder analysis, which is, I mean, it's no big deal. It's. It'll be a continuation of this conversation, but I will be just taking some notes on a grid. I wonder if I could borrow your. So, but my last question before that would be, so apart from. So what would your advice to others wishing to enter this space be? Apart from, you already spoken about the importance of being frugal, about being innovative, being patient. So apart from that, are there any tips?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: I think you should raise capital faster. I don't think we've done a very good job at that. Okay, you need to raise capital faster and you need to be very ambitious.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: When you're raising capital.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: You should be frugal when you spend it. But when you raise, you got to be very ambitious and gather as much as you can. You know, early on, we have, as early stage founders, very early stage founders, we have this thing about, you know, control and what percentage do I hold in the company? And you know, oh, why am I diluting? And, oh, I only own 30, 30% of the company. But, you know, 100% of zero is nothing. Right? So I think don't fall in that trap. Raise capital early, be frugal. You know, don't over promise, but be ambitious.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: It's like Mark Cuban says in the US Version of the original version of Shark, would you rather own 100% of a grape or 10% of a watermelon? Okay, wonderful. So in this next session, and this is the last section of the audio interview, we've made very good time. This is probably the shortest audio interview ever, and that is primarily because you've been just so effective in your communication. The other founders, they provide a great wealth of information but that doesn't matter the way they talk for a very long time. Okay, so the idea of the stakeholder analysis is to look at, you can, we can look at stakeholder groups. If you wish, you can name specific stakeholders in those groups as well. Like for example, if there's one government department, I should say government department, there's one funder that stands up. Or you can, you can talk about investors as a middle business segment. B2B2C. The middle B. So I will ask you, you know, it's a very simple framework. Perhaps it may seem an oversimplification, but it's just a framework. You get analysis. So I will ask you, what do they give you? What does the stakeholder give you? Okay, what do they get from you? Okay. And, and that is just the bare bones of the transaction. What do they give you? What do you give them?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: All right.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: And then above and beyond what you give them, the pure material aspect, is there something else that they've got from it, according to them, from their perspective?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: What else?

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: For example, this could be. Your customers may get clean cooking appliances, but they may come back to you and say that, you know, I, I get sick less often.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Yeah, yeah, sure.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Right. Therefore, that, that has improved my overall health and well being. And, or a woman may say that, you know, I, I felt very important when you came in and you gave me importance.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Sure.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: And you showed me the demo and it was my decision to buy. So that kind of thing is this. And then I will ask you, is this relationship. So how to what extent is this stakeholder relationship important for what you're doing in your business? And on a scale of 1 to 3, 1 being not so important, 3 being most important, and is it a positive, negative or neutral relationship? So those would be the five questions. So let's talk about.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Can we pause? I got a few calls in between.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Just want to make one of these? Sure, no worries. Okay, we're back. So before actually going to the questions, who would you define as your most important stakeholders or stakeholder groups?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: I think our customers are our most important stakeholders. And within that there's a whole classification of them. Because there are the end users who are our end customers. There are the dealers and distributors that we work through the channel, there are the offsetters who work with us to provide the prepay, and then There are other P2B players who are doing CSR kind of work or who may be in turn working with some of these other people to provide the stores.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Would you want to answer all five questions for Each of them, or do you want to treat it as one group?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: I would leave that decision to you. We could possibly pick two of them. So let's pick bulk buyers and let's pick individual buyers.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Right, That's a nice. Okay. Bulk buyers and customers. Individual buyers. Okay. Who else?

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: Okay, so these are the customers. These are important stakeholders. Of course, financing partners are important customers for us. Stakeholders.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Sorry, yeah.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: At different points in time, they can really affect our ability to work with end customers or not. Investors are definitely important stakeholders contractually as well. So employees are very important stakeholders because, yeah, if they don't work, we don't get anything done.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: And.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: You know, we haven't quite, we've tried to stay clear of policymakers, but they are important stakeholders nonetheless.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: Yeah, right. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Ankit
entries.text: I think that, that these groups and then, oh, yeah. And then one more important, I would say stakeholder group would be allied or associate enterprises. So given that we've been in business for so long, the companies that are roughly doing the same thing, or there are companies that my friends have started up in which I'm a shareholder or something of that sort, or the whole set of companies that NEHA and her team runs. So that is an important stakeholder. And then finally, vendors are an important stakeholder because they are critical to our manufacturing process. Process. Right. Because we, we run a whole manufacturing piece.

entries.speaker: Ganesh
entries.text: So it's quite a bit of stakeholders too. No worries. It's good to have a well rounded sort of approach.

entries.speaker: Ankit
